_meta: Context-minimal extraction. No schema, no commitment stores, no GDS. Raw turn segmentation with speech acts and candidate propositions asserted or conceded.

turns.speaker: Euthyphro
turns.content_summary: Asks why Socrates is at the King Archon's porch

turns.speaker: Both (alternating short exchanges)
turns.content_summary: Socrates explains Meletus's impiety charge. Euthyphro sympathizes, says he too is attacked for speaking about divine things.
propositions: Socrates is charged with impiety by Meletus
Meletus accuses Socrates of inventing new gods and denying old ones
Euthyphro claims special knowledge of divine things

turns.speaker: Both (alternating)
turns.content_summary: Euthyphro reveals he is prosecuting his own father for murder. Explains the circumstances: a dependent killed a slave; father bound and left him to die while consulting a diviner.
propositions: Euthyphro is prosecuting his father for murder
Pollution from murder is the same whether the victim is a relation or stranger
The real question is whether the killing was just or unjust
Euthyphro's family thinks it is impious for a son to prosecute his father

turns.speaker: Both
turns.content_summary: Socrates expresses surprise and asks if Euthyphro's knowledge of piety is so exact. Euthyphro claims exact knowledge is what distinguishes him.
propositions: Euthyphro claims exact knowledge of piety and impiety
This knowledge is what makes Euthyphro valuable/distinctive

turns.speaker: Both
turns.content_summary: Socrates asks Euthyphro to teach him what piety is — the general idea (eidos) that makes all pious things pious. Euthyphro agrees to try.
propositions: Piety is always the same in every action
Impiety is always the opposite of piety and the same with itself
There is one idea/form (eidos) that makes all pious things pious

turns.speaker: Euthyphro
turns.content_summary: Piety is doing as I do — prosecuting wrongdoers. Appeals to Zeus punishing Kronos as precedent.
propositions: Piety is prosecuting anyone guilty of murder or sacrilege, regardless of relation
Not prosecuting is impiety
The gods themselves act this way (Zeus bound Kronos)

turns.speaker: Both (Socratic questioning)
turns.content_summary: Socrates says that was only one example, not a definition. Asks for the general form. Euthyphro acknowledges there are many pious acts. Socrates asks for the nature of the idea itself.
propositions: An example is not a general definition
Socrates wants the general idea that makes all pious things pious — a standard by which to measure actions
Euthyphro concedes: there are many pious acts (his example is only one)

turns.speaker: Euthyphro
turns.content_summary: Piety is that which is dear to the gods; impiety is that which is not dear to them.
propositions: Piety is what is dear to the gods
Impiety is what is not dear to the gods

turns.speaker: Both (Socratic questioning leading to refutation)
turns.content_summary: Socrates builds an argument step by step: the gods have differences → differences about justice/good/evil cause enmity → so gods disagree about what is just → same act can be loved by some gods and hated by others → same act would be both pious and impious. Euthyphro concedes each step.
propositions: The gods have enmities, hatreds, and differences
Differences about numbers/magnitudes are resolved by counting/measuring
Differences about just/unjust, good/evil, honorable/dishonorable cannot be so easily resolved
The gods quarrel about the same things humans quarrel about (justice, good, evil)
Each god loves what he deems noble and just, and hates the opposite
The same things are regarded as just by some gods and unjust by others
The same things are therefore hated by the gods and loved by the gods
On this view, the same things would be both pious and impious

turns.speaker: Both
turns.content_summary: Euthyphro objects that all gods would agree murder is wrong. Socrates presses: gods may agree in general but disagree about specifics (was THIS killing unjust?). Socrates then proposes an amendment: what ALL the gods love is pious, what they ALL hate is impious.
propositions: Euthyphro claims all gods agree murder should be punished
Socrates: people and gods agree wrongdoers should be punished but disagree about WHO is the wrongdoer in specific cases
Socrates proposes amendment: what all the gods hate is impious, what they all love is pious

turns.speaker: Euthyphro
turns.content_summary: Euthyphro adopts the amendment: what all the gods love is pious and holy.
propositions: What all the gods love is pious and holy
What all the gods hate is impious

turns.speaker: Both (extended Socratic argument)
turns.content_summary: Socrates asks: is the pious loved by the gods because it is pious, or pious because it is loved? Through an extended analogy (carrying/being carried, seeing/being seen), establishes that the state follows the act. Piety is loved because it is pious (Euthyphro concedes). But being dear to the gods = being in a state of being loved (a consequence of being loved). So 'dear to the gods' and 'pious' are different things. Euthyphro has given an attribute (being loved), not the essence of piety.
propositions: A thing is in a state of being carried because it is carried, not carried because it is in that state
The state of being loved follows the act of being loved
Piety is loved by the gods because it is pious (not pious because it is loved)
What is dear to the gods is dear because it is loved by them (not loved because it is dear)
Therefore: 'dear to the gods' and 'pious/holy' are two different things
Euthyphro has offered an attribute of piety (being loved by the gods) and not the essence
Euthyphro admits: 'I really do not know how to express what I mean'

turns.speaker: Both (interlude)
turns.content_summary: Euthyphro says arguments walk away like Daedalus's statues. Socrates redirects: is not the pious necessarily just? Yes. Is all the just pious? No — piety is a part of justice.
propositions: The pious is necessarily just
Not all the just is pious (piety is a part of justice, not the whole)

turns.speaker: Both
turns.content_summary: Through the analogy of fear/reverence (reverence is a part of fear, not all fear is reverence), Socrates gets Euthyphro to say which part of justice piety is. Euthyphro: piety is that part of justice which attends to the gods.
propositions: Piety is that part of justice which attends to the gods
There is another part of justice which attends to men

turns.speaker: Both (Socratic questioning)
turns.content_summary: Socrates asks what 'attention' means. Through horse/dog/ox analogies: attention to X normally improves X. Does attention to the gods improve them? Euthyphro: no, certainly not. So what kind of attention?
propositions: Attention/care in the case of horses/dogs/oxen benefits and improves them
Pious acts do NOT make the gods better
Euthyphro retracts the 'care' meaning: 'that was certainly not what I meant'

turns.speaker: Both
turns.content_summary: Euthyphro shifts to 'service/ministration.' Socrates: service to what end? Medicine serves health; shipbuilding serves ships. What does service to the gods accomplish? Euthyphro evades the specific question and says piety is 'learning how to please the gods in word and deed, by prayers and sacrifices.'
propositions: Piety is a kind of service/ministration (hyperetike) to the gods
Every service has an end/purpose (health, ships, houses)
Euthyphro cannot name the specific work gods accomplish with our service
Piety is learning how to please the gods by prayers and sacrifices

turns.speaker: Both (Socratic reduction)
turns.content_summary: Socrates: so piety is a science of asking (prayer) and giving (sacrifice)? Giving what they want — but what do gods gain from our gifts? Not benefit. Euthyphro: 'honour' and 'what pleases them.' Socrates: so piety is what is pleasing/dear to the gods — we are back where we started.
propositions: Piety is a science of asking and giving
Giving to the gods what they want; asking from them what we want
Gods give us everything good; what do we give them?
Not benefit — 'tributes of honour' and 'what pleases them'
Piety is pleasing to the gods = what is dear to the gods
This is the same position as Definition 3, which was already shown to give only an attribute

turns.speaker: Both
turns.content_summary: Socrates states the argument has come round in a circle. Were we not saying holy ≠ loved by the gods? But now you are saying what is loved = holy. Either the former or the latter is wrong.
propositions: The argument comes round to the same point (explicit circularity)
Either the earlier refutation was wrong, or the current position is wrong
Euthyphro concedes: 'one of the two must be true'

turns.speaker: Both
turns.content_summary: Socrates asks to begin again. Euthyphro leaves: 'Another time, Socrates; for I am in a hurry.'
propositions: Socrates: we must begin again and ask what piety is
Euthyphro departs without resolution
